text: Hello, my name is Kristen Bremer and I'm a general surgery resident at Creighton University School of Medicine in Omaha, Nebraska. Today I will be presenting the technical aspects of robotic excision of a large epiphrenic diverticulum. We have no conflicts of interest.

An epiphrenic diverticulum is located in the distal esophagus above the diaphragm. These are typically caused by some type of esophageal motility disorder, which causes increased pressure on the wall of the esophagus, leading to diverticulum formation. They are false diverticula, more common on the right.

Our patient is a fifty-six-year-old male who reported regurgitation for the past twelve years, which was worse with laying on his left side. These symptoms had worsened over the past two years, with worsening regurgitation and the addition of dysphagia and recurrent aspiration. The patient underwent barium swallow, which showed a large, wide-mouthed diverticulum, eleven point five centimeters in size and the greatest dimension. An EGD showed food and fluid in the esophagus with a wide-mouthed epiphrenic diverticulum and a tight lower esophageal sphincter. The second photo showed the sphincter on the left and the diverticulum on the right, indicated by the yellow arrow. The third photo is a view inside this diverticulum after the debris was cleared out with multiple passes of a Roth net. Manometry showed a normal lower esophageal sphincter mean IRP as well as distal esophageal spasm.

All options, including endoscopic management of the diverticulum, were discussed with the patient. The patient elected to undergo a robotic-assisted laparoscopic epiphrenic diverticulectomy with hellermyotomy and dora fundoplication.

The surgical team uses three 8mm robotic ports, a 12mm robotic port for the stapler, and an 8mm assist port in the right lower quadrant, along with a liver retractor. The initial approach involves mobilizing the right crura and entering the mediastinum. The frenoesophageal membrane is fully mobilized, and the mediastinum and distal esophagus are mobilized up to 8 centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the epiphrenic diverticulum adherent to the pleura on the right side. A circumferential dissection of the diverticulum is performed, starting from the distal aspect. The pleura is gently peeled away from the diverticulum as the dissection progresses anteriorly toward the pericardium and posteriorly.

The surgeon uses endoscopy to outline the diverticulum in relation to surrounding structures. Blunt dissection techniques are employed to remove adhesions between the diverticulum and adjacent tissues. The robotic-assisted camera provides stable visualization as the mediastinal dissection progresses, focusing on the proximal aspect of the diverticulum.

The surgeon completes the separation of the diverticulum from surrounding structures and delivers it out of the mediastinum. The neck of the diverticulum is identified, extending from five to nine centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The diverticulectomy is performed using multiple endo-GI blue loads, with an endoscope used as a bougie. The stapled area is then imbricated using vicryl sutures.

The surgeon performs a myotomy on the anterolateral wall, extending two centimeters onto the stomach and five centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction, near the lower border of the epiphrenic diverticulum. The crura are approximated using interrupted silk sutures, followed by an anterior Dor fundoplication.
Intraoperative endoscopy reveals a well-opened gastroesophageal junction with a visible fundoplication valve, confirming the absence of mucosal injury.
The divided diverticulum is visible being removed from the surgical field using an endobag.

In conclusion, a large epiphrenic diverticulectomy can be performed safely using a transabdominal approach. Visualization within the mediastinum has significantly improved with utilization of a robotic platform. Thank you.
